title: Show inline validation states on form buttons
summary: Models place submit buttons at the bottom of forms without indicating whether the form is valid, loading, or has errors — users click submit, see no feedback, and must scroll up to discover missing fields, compounding task time.
instruction: Submit buttons must visually indicate the form's state: disabled and gray when the form has validation errors (with a tooltip or inline message), show a loading spinner during submission, and enable with subtle emphasis when the form is valid. Do not rely on a popover or toast at the top of the page to communicate form validity.
rationale: Forms where the user fills fields, clicks submit, scrolls back up to see errors, corrects, and submits again create a frustrating loop. Inline button state changes provide immediate feedback near the user's action point, reducing form completion time and error recovery effort.
violations: A long checkout form where clicking 'Place Order' scrolls to the top to show a validation error banner, but the button never changes state — user must scroll down, hunt for errors, and click again.
A registration form with a submit button that appears active even with invalid fields — user clicks, nothing happens (client-side validation prevents submit), with no visual feedback.
A form where the submit button shows a loading state only after a 1-second delay, leaving the user uncertain whether the click registered.
controls: A checkout form with a 'Place Order' button that is disabled (gray, reduced opacity) while any required field is invalid, with an inline message '2 required fields incomplete' directly above the button.

A registration form with a button that shows 'Complete your profile' when fields are empty, changes to 'Ready to join' (with a gentle emphasis pulse) when all fields are valid, and shows a spinner with 'Creating account…' upon click.
A multi-step form where the 'Continue' button is disabled with a tooltip 'Required fields in this section incomplete' on hover, enabling only when all visible fields pass validation.
borderlineCases: A search form where the submit button is always active and the page handles empty queries gracefully — the button state is static because the action is always valid.
contextualExceptions: Forms submitted to external services where immediate client-side validation is not possible (e.g., credit card authorization that requires a server round-trip).
Forms with a single field that has no validation requirement (e.g., a simple search box).
detectorSignal: Form element with a submit button that has no disabled state, no loading state class, and no dynamic content change between valid/invalid states
matcherProposal: Identify all <form> elements or [role=form] containers. Collect submit buttons (input[type=submit], button[type=submit], [role=button] with submit context). Check for: (a) [disabled] attribute or class, (b) CSS classes indicating loading state, (c) aria-busy attribute, (d) dynamic text content that changes with state. Flag if none detected.
detectorLimitations: Cannot detect live form states from a static capture — an always-enabled button may be functionally correct if client-side validation is absent and the form accepts incomplete submissions gracefully.
Inline validation messages above the button (not on the button) provide the same function but may not be detected as button state.
Progressive enhancement patterns where button state changes are applied via JavaScript class toggles that are not visible in the initial render.
falsePositiveScenarios: A simple contact form with email-only validation where the button is always enabled and invalid email throws an inline error next to the input — the button does not need to change state.
A form submitted via AJAX where the button shows a spinner via CSS pseudo-element on a class added by JavaScript — not detectable in static HTML without runtime state.
mitigationStrategy: Only flag forms with 3+ required inputs where the submit button has no visible state differentiation. Exclude single-field forms and search forms. Lower to warning.

fixturePlans.violation: A checkout form (10 fields) with a 'Place Order' button that is always bright blue and clickable — clicking reveals errors at the top of the page with no button state feedback.
fixturePlans.control: A checkout form where the 'Place Order' button shows '2 fields incomplete' in muted gray with reduced opacity when validation fails, enables in brand blue when valid, and shows a spinner+ 'Processing…' on click.
fixturePlans.borderline: A three-field contact form with a submit button that is always active — valid because client-side validation throws inline errors next to individual fields and the button state is static.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
overlapNotes: Second forms proposal, distinct from P-A-022 (label placement). No overlap with existing rules.
